title: Coconut wax travel tin candle
subtitle: A small, lidded tin candle in natural coconut wax with a calming fragrance
excerpt: Coconut wax produces a smooth, creamy-white finish without the rough surface that soy wax can develop when it cools quickly. The lidded tin makes the candle portable and protects the wax during storage. A single CD-8 wick suits the 7 cm diameter of a standard 200 ml tin.
sourceNotes: Coconut wax behaviour from The Candle Maker's Store guide (thecandlemakersstore.co.uk, May 2026). Fragrance load and pour temperature from coconut wax supplier datasheets. Tin candle wick sizing from CandleScience wick guide.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 travel tin candle (~130 g wax in a 200 ml lidded tin)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Each tin takes approximately 130 g wax. Use the same tin diameter across a batch so one wick size applies to all.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for at least 3 days before the first burn for the best scent throw. The smooth surface of coconut wax sets fully over 24 hours.
recipe.temperatureNote: Add fragrance at 60 degrees Celsius. Pour at 55 to 60 degrees Celsius. Coconut wax poured above 65 degrees Celsius can sink in the centre as it cools.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years. Keep the lid on when not in use to slow fragrance evaporation and protect the wax surface. Store away from direct sunlight and heat sources.

recipeTools.notes: Or two lolly sticks held across the tin rim with tape

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of fragrance or essential oil by weight relative to the wax. Coconut wax holds 6 to 8% fragrance by weight. This formula uses 7%.
The first burn of a candle, which sets the maximum melt pool diameter for all subsequent burns. A full first burn prevents tunnelling.

content.text: Coconut wax is made from hydrogenated coconut oil. It produces a smooth, creamy surface without the slight roughness that can appear on soy candles as they cool. The higher melt point of coconut wax relative to soy means the finished candle is a little more resistant to warm weather: a useful property for a travel tin that may be packed in a bag. The
for this formula is 7% of wax weight: a blend of lavender and sweet orange essential oils that works in a bedroom or bathroom.

The tin format suits coconut wax because tins conduct heat evenly, helping the wax reach the edges on the first burn. A CD-8 wick is correct for a 7 cm diameter tin. If the wick goes out in the first 30 minutes or the melt pool does not reach the edges within 2 hours, step up to a CD-10 on the next batch.

items.prepNote: 4.6% of wax weight; the base note
2.3% of wax weight; adds brightness. Mild phototoxicity: not a concern in a candle context
CD-8 for a 7 cm diameter tin

content.text: Fix the pre-tabbed wick to the centre of the tin base using a small dot of melted wax or wick sticker. Hold it in place with a centring bar or two lolly sticks taped across the tin rim.

Melt the coconut wax in the pouring pitcher over a simmering-water bath. Remove from heat when fully liquid. Allow to cool to 60 degrees Celsius. Stir in both essential oils. Pour slowly into the tin at 55 to 60 degrees Celsius, keeping the wick centred. Leave undisturbed at room temperature for a minimum of 12 hours. The surface will set smooth. Trim the wick to 5 mm before the first burn.
to melt the wax fully to the tin edge before extinguishing. For a 7 cm tin this takes around 2 hours. Never burn for more than 4 hours at a time and always burn on a heat-resistant surface.
2 years. Keep the lid on between uses and store away from heat and direct sunlight. Coconut wax resists warming better than soy wax but will still soften in a hot car or in direct summer sun.
